answer: Across `unknown-manusmrti-347b76` chapter 7, `danda` is presented as the king’s divinely grounded power of punishment and social order: it is created by the Lord as Brahma’s own radiance and as Dharma itself; it protects all beings, keeps the world in order through fear, and must be applied with due consideration, otherwise it destroys everything (`unknown-manusmrti-347b76`, ch. 7, esp. Mn_7.14-19, 22, 25-31). In the parallel material from `unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8` chapter 3, `danda` is the dark, red-eyed rod of justice under which subjects prosper when the ruler judges well; the chapter emphasizes proportionate, just punishment and that no one is exempt if they depart from their own dharma (`unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8`, ch. 3, Vi_3.91-95). In `unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6` chapter 1, the parallel treatment makes `danda` both a political expedient (`daṇḍa` as force, the last of the four expedients) and, more importantly, Brahma-created punishment for disciplining the wicked; it may be wielded only by a truthful, pure, wise, well-assisted king, and just punishment brings heaven, fame, and victory, while unjust punishment destroys them. Yajnavalkya also specifies its forms: censure, verbal rebuke, fine, and corporal punishment, to be imposed according to offense and circumstance (`unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6`, ch. 1, Yj_1.346, 354-357, 367-368).
